title: Psalms 6
subtitle: A Cry in Anguish

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “LORD, Do Not Rebuke Me in Your Anger”

heb.gloss: be gracious to me / have mercy
heb.paragraph: "LORD, have mercy (ḥānnēnî) on me, for I am faint" (v.2). The first penitential psalm. David is physically broken: bones troubled, soul in anguish. The plea is not for justice but for mercy — grace when you deserve judgment.

refs.note: "LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger" — the same opening, the same penitential posture.
"The Lord disciplines the one he loves" — the NT theology of divine correction.

notes.note: "LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger. Have mercy on me, for I am faint; heal me, for my bones are in agony." The plea combines confession (implicit: I deserve rebuke) with appeal (explicit: have mercy).
"My soul is in deep anguish. How long, LORD, how long? Turn, LORD, and deliver me." The "how long?" is not rebellion but anguished faith. He asks God to turn — implying God has turned away.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "my bones are shaken" — the physical vocabulary is not metaphorical. The psalmist’s body participates in his spiritual anguish. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: David asks for moderated discipline, not removal. Calvin: the request acknowledges God’s right to rebuke and asks only for restraint in its intensity. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the parallel between "anger" and "hot displeasure" uses two different words for divine wrath, intensifying the plea.
The NET: "among the dead no one proclaims your name" — in Sheol, there is no worship. This is David’s argument for staying alive: the dead cannot praise.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the first penitential psalm. David does not deny deserving rebuke; he asks that it be moderated. The plea is: discipline me, but not in fury. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the seven penitential psalms form a category the early church used in Lent. Psalm 6 is the first: raw, physical, urgent. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "how long?" is asked over 20 times in the Psalter. It is the default question of suffering faith: not "why?" but "how much longer?". Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: The first of seven penitential psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). David is physically and spiritually broken. His bones are troubled (v.2), his soul is in anguish (v.3). The question: "how long, LORD, how long?" (v.3) — the Psalter’s recurring cry of impatience in suffering.

sections.header: Verses 6–10 — “The LORD Has Heard My Weeping”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD has heard (shāmaʿ) my cry for mercy" (v.9). The pivot from despair to confidence. David was drowning in tears (v.6) — flooding his bed, drenching his couch. Then the sudden turn: the LORD has heard. Past tense. The prayer was answered before the psalm ended.

refs.note: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit" — the theology underlying Psalm 6’s resolution.
"He will wipe every tear from their eyes" — the final answer to David’s weeping.

notes.note: "I am worn out from my groaning. All night long I flood my bed with weeping." Physical exhaustion from grief. The body processes sorrow.
"Away from me, all you who do evil, for the LORD has heard my weeping." The turn. Confidence breaks through despair. Jesus quotes this in Matthew 7:23.
"All my enemies will be overwhelmed with shame and anguish." The reversal: what David experienced (shame, anguish) will be redirected to his enemies.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "away from me" is a dismissal of enemies spoken with sudden authority. The weeping man becomes the commanding man in a single breath. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: the "LORD has heard" is the pivot on which the entire psalm turns. Calvin: prayer changes the one who prays before it changes the circumstances. David is the same person, in the same danger, but now confident. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "flood my bed" uses the verb sahâ (to swim/overflow). The image is of tears literally pooling on the bed. Ancient sleep was on the floor — tears would collect.
The NET: Jesus echoes this verse in Matt 7:23 ("away from me, you evildoers"). The dismissal of evildoers from God’s presence is both Davidic and christological.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the sudden turn from weeping to command is one of the Psalter’s characteristic moves. Faith does not wait for evidence; it declares what it has heard from God. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the abrupt shift from lament to confidence is the "certainty of being heard" — a feature of biblical laments where the psalmist receives inner assurance mid-prayer. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "flood my bed with weeping" is hyperbole that communicates emotional truth. The exaggeration IS the accuracy — grief feels like drowning. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David’s tears are physical: he floods his bed, drenches his couch with weeping, his eyes grow weak with sorrow (vv.6–7). Then the dramatic turn: "Away from me, all you who do evil, for the LORD has heard my weeping" (v.8). From tears to triumph in one verse. The enemies will be ashamed and dismayed (v.10). The lament’s resolution comes not through changed circumstances but through the conviction that God heard.

ppl.text: David is physically and spiritually broken…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: He asks God to turn — implying God has turned away…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAway from me, all you who do evil, for the Lord has heard my weeping.ESVDepart from me, all you workers of evil, for the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping.NIVHave mercy on me, Lord, for I am faint; heal me, Lord, for my bones are in agony.ESVBe gracious to me, O LORD, for I am languishing; heal me, O LORD, for my bones are troubled.

rec.text: ""LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger. Have mercy on me, for I am faint; heal me, for my bones are in agony." The plea combines confession (implicit: I deserve rebuke) with appeal (explicit: have merc…"

rows.label: The Anguish — 6:1–5
rows.text: "How long, LORD?" Bones troubled, soul in anguish. The first penitential psalm.

hebtext.gloss: be gracious to me / have mercy
hebtext.note: "LORD, have mercy (ḥānnēnî) on me, for I am faint" (v.2). The first penitential psalm. David is physically broken: bones troubled, soul in anguish. The plea is not for justice but for mercy — grace when you deserve judgment.

"The LORD has heard (shāmaʿ) my cry for mercy" (v.9). The pivot from despair to confidence. David was drowning in tears (v.6) — flooding his bed, drenching his couch. Then the sudden turn: the LORD has heard. Past tense. The prayer was answered before the psalm ended.

thread.text: "LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger" — the same opening, the same penitential posture.
"The Lord disciplines the one he loves" — the NT theology of divine correction.
"The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit" — the theology underlying Psalm 6’s resolution.
"He will wipe every tear from their eyes" — the final answer to David’s weeping.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: the plea for grace when you deserve judgment. Faith: the sudden turn from despair to confidence. The first penitential psalm.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on this cry of anguish — bones trembling, soul in agony — consider asking God whether you give yourself permission to bring Him prayers that are more sob than sentence, trusting that He hears what words cannot carry.
